summary:
The Mushabian culture (or Mushabian complex) was an Epipaleolithic hunter-gatherer tradition of the Negev Desert, Sinai Peninsula, and adjacent areas, dated approximately 13,000–10,500 BCE. Named after the Mushabi sites in the Sinai, the Mushabian is identified by a distinctive microlithic tool industry with characteristic backed and retouched bladelets and geometric forms that differ from the contemporaneous [[event:4|Natufian culture]] of the Mediterranean zone to the north.

The Mushabian is associated with mobile, small-band foragers adapted to the arid and hyper-arid zones of the southern Levant and Sinai, exploiting game animals, plant foods, and possibly coastal marine resources along the Red Sea and Mediterranean shores. The origins of the Mushabian are debated — some researchers have proposed connections to North African Epipaleolithic traditions that entered the Sinai and Negev from Egypt, representing a northward expansion of African populations, while others see it as a local development from earlier Levantine Epipaleolithic traditions. The Mushabian is succeeded by the [[event:1334|Harifian culture]] in the Negev and Sinai as one of the last mobile forager traditions of the southern Levant before the region was gradually integrated into the broader Neolithic cultural sphere.
